items.myth: AUC is the accuracy of the model.
items.reality: AUC measures ranking across all thresholds, not the fraction correct at one cutoff. A model with mediocre accuracy at 0.5 can have excellent AUC if scores order classes well.
items.example: AUC 0.88 with 82% accuracy at 0.5 — both numbers are valid but answer different questions.

items.reality: Any real-valued score that ranks positives higher works — SVM decision_function, raw logits, or graded outputs. Probabilities are convenient, not mandatory.
items.example: roc_auc_score(y_test, svm.decision_function(X_test)) is standard and correct.

items.myth: A higher AUC always means a better model for deployment.
items.reality: AUC ignores the exact FPR at the TPR you need and ignores calibration. A slightly lower-AUC model may win on precision at your operating point or on inference cost.
items.example: Model B: AUC 0.89, precision 0.6 at required recall. Model A: AUC 0.91, precision 0.35 at same recall — deploy B.

items.myth: AUC = 0.5 means the model is always wrong.
items.reality: AUC 0.5 means ranking is no better than random — predictions are uninformative for ordering positives above negatives, not necessarily always misclassified at 0.5.
items.example: Inverted scores (negatives ranked higher) yield AUC < 0.5; flipping the sign gives AUC > 0.5.
